data.title: Bank of Queensland Target Price Raised 6.5% to A$6.60/Share by Morgan Stanley>BOQ.AU
COG Financial Target Price Raised 3.0% to A$1.38/Share by Ord Minnett>COG.AU
White House Says China Faces Up to 245% Tariff
data.description:
The White House clarified that China now faces tariffs of up to 245% on imports of goods into the US "as a result of its retaliatory actions."

This includes a 125% reciprocal tariff, a 20% tariff to address the fentanyl crisis, and Section 301 tariffs on specific goods ranging between 7.5% and 100%, according to a White House fact sheet published Tuesday.

Prior to the recent announcement, tit-for-tat tariffs saw the US slapping duties of 145% on Chinese goods and China placing duties of 125% on US goods.

data.title: Iluka Resources Target Price Raised 9.3% to A$5.90/Share by Ord Minnett>ILU.AU
Singapore's Non-Oil Domestic Exports Rise 5.4% In March
data.description:
Singapore's non-oil domestic exports or the NODX, was up 5.4% year over year in March, easing from a 7.6% increase in the previous month, according to data released by the Enterprise SG on Thursday.

NODX grew 3.3% during the first quarter of the year.

The NODX growth was driven by a 11.9% increase in electronic NODX, with PCs, disk media products and ICs contributing to the growth, the data showed.

Non-oil re-exports or the NORX was up 5.5% year over year in March, moderating from a 12.7% growth in the previous month.

The growth was boosted by a 9.2% in electronic NORX.

Total trade increased by 3.4% year over year in March, following a 4.6% rise in the previous month.

Total exports were up 2.3% in March compared to 5.4% in the previous month. Oil exports declined by 12.4% while non-oil exports were up 5.5%.

data.title: Kingsoft Cloud Hong Kong shares drop on discounted share sale
data.description:
** Hong Kong shares of Kingsoft Cloud drop 8.9% to HK$5.82, their lowest since April 7, and on course for the third consecutive session of decline

** U.S.-listed stock plunged 16.8% on Wednesday

** Beijing-based cloud service provider sold 18.5 mln American depositary shares on Wednesday in its stock offering at $11.27 apiece for proceeds of $208.5 mln

** Offering priced at a 14.6% discount to stock's last close

** Controlling shareholder Kingsoft Corp agreed to purchase 69.38 mln Kingsoft Cloud shares in a private placement at HK$5.83 each, raising HK$404.5 mln ($52.11 mln)

** Proceed from the deals will be used for upgrading and expanding infrastructure, investments in technology and product development - Kingsoft Cloud

** Shares of Kingsoft Corp rise 2.3% to HK$36.15, snapping two straight sessions of fall

** Hang Seng Tech Index rises 1.6%, Hang Seng Index climbs 1.2%

** Kingsoft Cloud shares up 1.9% YTD

($1 = 7.7618 Hong Kong dollars)

data.description:

data.title: China says it will ignore US 'tariff numbers game'
data.description:
China will pay no attention if the United States continues to play the "tariff numbers game", China's foreign ministry said on Thursday, after the White House outline how China faces tariffs of up to 245% due to its retaliatory actions.

In a fact sheet released on Tuesday, the White House said China's total duties include the latest reciprocal tariff of 125%, a 20% tariff to address the fentanyl crisis, and tariffs of between 7.5% and 100% on specific goods to address unfair trade practices.

U.S. President Donald Trump announced additional tariffs on all countries two weeks ago, before suddenly rolling back higher "reciprocal tariffs" for dozens of countries while keeping punishing duties on China.

Beijing raised its own levies on U.S. goods in response and has not sought talks, which it says can only be conducted on the basis of mutual respect and equality. Meanwhile, many other nations have begun looking at bilateral deals with Washington.

Last week, China also filed a new complaint with the World Trade Organization expressing "grave concern" over U.S. tariffs, accusing Washington of violating the global trade body's rules.

China this week unexpectedly appointed a new trade negotiator who would be key in any talks to resolve the escalating tariff war, replacing trade tsar Wang Shouwen with Li Chenggang, its envoy to the WTO.

Washington said Trump was open to making a trade deal with China but Beijing should make the first move, insisting that China needed "our money".

data.title: Australia Added Jobs in March, Though Unemployment Ticked Up
data.description:
By James Glynn and Mike Cherney

SYDNEY--Australia added jobs in March, though the unemployment rate ticked higher from the previous month.

The unemployment rate was 4.1% in March, up from a revised 4.0% in February, the Australian Bureau of Statistics said Thursday. Still, the economy added 32,200 jobs, with 15,000 of those full-time positions.

A relatively tight job market may keep the Reserve Bank of Australia from cutting interest rates at its next policy meeting in late May.

Still, with the focus shifting to how the world economy will fare amid a worsening trade war, some economists expect the RBA will cut interest rates by 50 basis points next month.

Write to James Glynn at james.glynn@wsj.com and Mike Cherney at mike.cherney@wsj.com

data.title: Shares in Hong Kong Stock Rally
data.description:
Hong Kong's equities climbed 266 points or 1.3% to 21,324 in early trade Thursday, lifted by broad-based sector gains.

The rebound followed a weak prior session and came amid a surge in U.S. futures after Wall Street’s sell-off overnight, which was triggered by Fed Chair Powell’s warning that trade tensions could hinder the central bank’s inflation and employment goals.

Meanwhile, mainland markets attempted to extend a four-day rise after upbeat Q1 GDP data, even as authorities prepared for the impact of U.S. tariffs.

State-backed investors reportedly stepped in Wednesday afternoon, with trading volumes spiking in some ETFs tracking key indexes.

Separately, Premier Li Qiang called for stronger efforts to boost consumption and domestic demand.

However, gains were capped by uncertainty over potential new tariffs, as investors struggled to navigate shifting tit-for-tat developments.

Among top performers were Longfor Group (5.5%), KE Holdings (5.2%), SMIC (3.2%), and Tencent Hlds. (2.5%).

data.title: Nam Cheong Secures Additional Vessel Chartering Contracts Worth MYR317 Million; Shares Jump 9%
data.description:
Nam Cheong secured multi-year offshore support vessel charter contracts worth around 317.1 million Malaysian ringgit, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Wednesday.

The contracts are from regional oil companies in Malaysia and include seven units of anchor handling tug supply vessels that will be deployed in Malaysia and Thailand.

With the new contracts, the company now has 21 vessels under long-term charter, representing 56.8% of its total fleet.

Shares of the offshore support vessel provider surged over 9% in recent trading.

Market Chatter: WiseTech Faces Formal ASIC Probe into Disclosure Practices, CEO's Share Trading
data.description:
WiseTech Global is under formal investigation by the Australian Securities and Investments Commission over founder and Chief Executive Richard White's share trading during a blackout period and the company's handling of market disclosures related to misconduct allegations, according to a Thursday report by the Australian Financial Review.

The ASIC is looking into whether White engaged in insider trading by selling about AU$229 million worth of shares between Dec. 24, 2024, and Feb. 19, a period when WiseTech's trading policy forbade such transactions ahead of its earnings release, the report said.

The probe also includes scrutiny of whether WiseTech met its continuous disclosure obligations during a turbulent period marked by internal investigations into White's conduct, the report added.

WiseTech and ASIC did not immediately respond to MT Newswires' request for comment.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: South32 Cuts Production Outlook for Giant Australian Silver, Lead MineUpdate
data.description:
By Rhiannon Hoyle

Australian miner South32 said production will be lower than previously envisaged this year at its Cannington mine, one of the world's largest sources of silver and lead.

The company downgraded its fiscal 2025 production guidance for the operation by 10% on Thursday, citing challenging geotechnical conditions that made it harder to access higher-grade areas of the mine.

Output from the underground mining operation, which also produces zinc, fell by 27% from a year earlier in the three months through March, to 50,200 metric tons of zinc equivalent. Production was 37% below the previous quarter.

The Cannington mine, located on a former sheep and cattle station in Australia's Queensland state, is managing "increased underground activity and complexity," South32 said.

The company now expects full-year production of 239,200 tons of zinc equivalent there. It produced 285,200 tons in the previous fiscal year.

South32 also expects the mine's annual operating costs to be roughly 11% higher than previously forecast because it is processing less ore.

"Looking forward, we are completing work to assess optimal underground mining rates and stope sequencing to manage continued geotechnical challenges and unlock value over the remaining mine life at Cannington," the company said.

With the exception of Cannington, South32's quarterly output of commodities including copper and aluminum was broadly in line with market estimates, analysts said.

The company said third-quarter copper-equivalent production at the Sierra Gorda mine in Chile rose by 19% from a year earlier, while aluminum production in the Brazil Aluminium business rose by 38% over the same period.

"Looking ahead, our focus on operating discipline, active cost management and a strong balance sheet leaves us well positioned to manage a period of potential uncertainty in global markets," Chief Executive Graham Kerr said.

Write to Rhiannon Hoyle at rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com

data.title: China Stocks Hold Steady as Trade Talks Eyed
data.description:
The Shanghai Composite held above 3,270 while the Shenzhen Component rose 0.3% to 9,800 on Thursday, with mainland stocks remaining supported following reports that China is open to engage in trade negotiations with the US with certain preconditions.

Beijing is reportedly seeking greater respect, policy consistency, and a designated US negotiator with full backing from President Trump, as a prerequisite for talks.

Confusion arose after a White House statement indicated that certain Chinese goods may face tariffs of up to 245%, encompassing both new and historical levies dating back to the first Trump administration.

However, markets were calmed somewhat by clarification that the new tariffs stand at 145%.

Technology shares led the gains, with Talkweb Information surging 10%, Cambricon Technologies rising 3.4%, and Shanghai Belling adding 3.5%.

data.title: Asia Enterprises Receives Listing and Quotation Notice for 28.4 Million Shares
data.description:
Asia Enterprises received a listing and quotation notice from the SGX for 28,400,000 consideration shares, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Wednesday.

In an earlier filing, the company had agreed to acquire 385,000 shares in GKE Metal Logistics from Hung Ling Holding for SG$8.9 million.

The total shares represent 28.64% of GKE Metal Logistics.

The consideration for the proposed acquisition will be carried out through the allotment and issuance of 28,400,000 shares in Asia Enterprises' capital at an issue price of SG$0.312 per consideration share.

GKE Metal is engaged in the business of provision of storage, freight forwarding, warehousing, packing, removal and delivery services.

data.title: CapitaLand Malaysia Trust's Industrial Segment Growth Appears on TrackMarket Talk
data.description: CapitaLand Malaysia Trust appears to be on track to expand its industrial segment contribution to 20% of its portfolio income within three years, up from the current level of under 5%, Kenanga IB analyst Chris Tong says in a note. He says the company's performance will be supported by continued strength from northern retail assets and rising interest from foreign tenants amid U.S.-China trade tensions. With high expectations for developments in Penang, he foresees the positive trajectory continuing into 2025-2026. Kenanga maintains an outperform rating on CapitaLand and holds a target price of MYR0.75, citing an attractive dividend yield higher than 7% and a resilient earnings outlook. Shares are 1.6% higher at MYR0.64. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Bank of Korea Gov: Decision to Hold Rates Steady Wasn't Unanimous
BOK Gov: One Dissenting Board Member Called For Rate Cut
South Korean Won Declines on Stronger Dollar
data.description:
The South Korean won declined to around 1,420 per dollar on Thursday, reversing gains from the previous session as the US dollar strengthened following remarks from the Federal Reserve.

On Wednesday, Fed Chair Jerome Powell indicated that the Fed is in no hurry to cut interest rates, while emphasizing that tariffs could stoke inflation and dampen economic growth.

In South Korea, the central bank kept its base rate steady at 2.75% during its April 2025 meeting, in line with expectations.

The rate remains at its lowest level since September 2022, as the Bank of Korea continues its efforts to stabilize inflation, support sluggish economic growth, and mitigate risks from global economic uncertainties, while also navigating the political landscape ahead of the country's upcoming presidential election on June 3, 2025.

data.title: Market Chatter: Limited Impact Expected For South Korean Chipmakers from New US Nvidia Export Restrictions
data.description:
South Korean chipmakers are expected to witness minimal impact from new US restrictions on Nvidia's H20 AI accelerator exports to China, Yonhap News reported Wednesday, citing analysts.

While SK Hynix supplies HBM3E memory for the H20 chips, it has already locked in sales contracts and is now focusing on more advanced 12-layer HBM3E chips for non-Chinese markets. Samsung Electronics is not involved in H20-related HBM supply, the report said.

Nvidia, meanwhile, anticipates a $5.5 billion hit due to the licensing requirement now imposed by US authorities on H20 exports, according to the report.

Disclaimer

data.title: Copper Lower Amid Tariff ConcernsMarket Talk
data.description: Copper is lower amid tariff concerns. Despite China's solid 1Q GDP data, 2Q activity will be affected by the uncertainty in U.S. tariff policy, according to ANZ research analysts in a commentary. Investors' expectations for risks and a potential recession are rising again, Nanhua Futures analysts say, adding that the sentiment may continue to intensify in the short term. The three-month LME copper contract is 0.3% lower at $9,177.00 a ton. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Times - April 17
data.description:
The following are the top stories in the Financial Times. Reuters has not verified these stories and does not vouch for their accuracy.

Headlines

- Gerry Cardinale's RedBird evaluating takeover of UK's Telegraph newspaper

- Trump administration halts Equinor's $5 bln New York energy project

- British Steel's Chinese owner says UK government must 'respect' its rights

- Lyft buys European taxi app FreeNow in 175 mln euros deal

Overview

- Gerry Cardinale's RedBird Capital Partners is drawing up plans to take control of The Telegraph in an attempt to bring an end to the protracted two-year sale of the British newspaper group.

- The U.S has halted a $5 billion offshore wind project that Norway's Equinor is developing, dealing a blow to the embattled renewable power sector and signalling an aggressive new push against clean energy initiatives.

- The Chinese owner of British Steel, Jingye British Steel, has called on the UK government to "respect and protect" its rights as a foreign investor after ministers dramatically seized control of the company over the weekend.

- Lyft is expanding its ride-hailing business across the Atlantic for the first time with a 175 million-euro ($199 million) acquisition of European taxi app FreeNow.

($1 = 0.8794 euros)

data.title: Bank of Korea Keeps Rates on Hold as Tariff Uncertainty RisesUpdate
data.description:
By Kwanwoo Jun

South Korea's central bank held interest rates steady in April, taking a widely anticipated breather as it gauges how best to support an economy facing a severe hit from U.S. tariffs.

The Bank of Korea kept its benchmark seven-day repurchase rate unchanged at 2.75% on Thursday, pausing its easing cycle after making cuts in February this year, and in October and November of 2024.

Twenty out of 25 economists surveyed by The Wall Street Journal ahead of the decision had forecast no rate change in April, expecting uncertainty over President Trump's erratic trade announcements to make BOK policymakers cautious. The central bank likely also wants more time to assess the impact of prior rate cuts before making any more moves. Most economists forecast a cut could resume in May.

Asia's fourth-largest economy relies on exports for nearly half of its gross domestic product growth, leaving it exposed to trade disruptions from the sweeping U.S. tariffs announced recently.

Central banks in New Zealand, Singapore, India and the Philippines have recently eased policy settings, citing uncertainty around how U.S. trade policy will affect the global economy.

Asia stands to be particularly hard-hit by a slowdown in trade, and many economists have lowered growth outlooks for export-reliant countries like South Korea.

South Korea's state-run think tank cut its growth forecast for the country this year in February, citing the risk of new Trump tariffs. Downgrades from the likes of JPMorgan and Citigroup have followed, and the BOK is widely expected to lower the outlook for the country again in May after having cut its 2025 GDP growth forecast in February to 1.5% from 1.9%.

South Korean exports in March grew more strongly than expected, showing resilience and partly being helped by front-loaded shipments ahead of tariff hikes. But the slew of announcements from the Trump's administration since then has darkened the outlook for global growth and trade.

South Korean policymakers recently rolled out emergency measures--including billions of dollars for key industries--to help ease the impact of Trump tariffs. They have warned that the trade restrictions will cause significant damage to national exports like cars, semiconductors and electronics.

The Trump administration has imposed a 25% levy on all vehicles shipped to the U.S., effective since early April, and a duty on auto parts that is set to kick in later.

South Korea's shipped around $35 billion worth of cars to the U.S. in 2024, accounting for nearly half of its total auto exports, according to the trade ministry.

Last week, the government in Seoul announced a multi-billion-dollar package for the auto sector, which includes more financial aid, tax breaks and subsidies for carmakers. It is also planning to increase support for chip makers to around $23 billion, proposing a bigger-than-expected supplementary budget.

On top of sector tariffs, Trump also announced a separate 25% reciprocal tariff on South Korea, but has paused the measure for 90 days to allow for trade negotiations.

Capital Economics senior Asia economist Gareth Leather said Thursday that the BOK's latest move "represents a pause not an end to the easing cycle" given that the country's first-quarter GDP growth likely remained weak due to a political crisis over its impeached president and the property-sector downturn.

He forecasts below-consensus 0.9% growth for the country this year.

"The economy is certainly in need of more support," Leather said. "The drag from tariffs and the uncertainty created by Trump's trade policy will further weigh on demand this year."

Write to Kwanwoo Jun at kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com

data.title: Market Chatter: South Korea, US Set for High-Level Tariff Talks Amid 90-Day Trade Truce
data.description:
South Korea's Finance Minister Choi Sang-mok will meet with US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent next week to discuss Washington's new import tariffs, Yonhap News reported Wednesday, citing the finance ministry.

The talks, proposed by Bessent, will take place on the sidelines of the G20 finance ministers' and central bank governors' meeting.

The meeting follows the United States' move to impose steep reciprocal tariffs on key trade partners, including South Korea, though a 90-day suspension has been granted for negotiations.

Choi emphasized that Seoul won't make a hasty deal, noting that final decisions will be left to the incoming administration, the report said.

Disclaimer
